title: Charmed Life - Chapter 5 (Fragment 3)
text:
Neither Cat nor Gwendolen had any money beside their crown pieces. They had to go away without giving him anything. The boy shouted after them.

“Stuck-up little witch! Mingy little warlock!”

Gwendolen did not mind this in the least, but Cat was so ashamed that he wanted to go back and explain.

Mr. Baslam lived in a shabby cottage with an ill-written notice propped in one window: Eggsotick
 Serplys. Gwendolen looked at it rather pityingly as she hammered on the door with the dingy knocker. When Mr. Baslam opened his door, he proved to be a fat person in old trousers which sagged to make room for his fatness, and with red, drooping eyes like a St. Bernard’s. He started to shut the door again as soon as he saw them.

“Not today, thank you,” he said, and a strong smell of beer came out with the words.

“Mr. Nostrum sent me,” said Gwendolen. “Mr. William Nostrum.”

The door stopped shutting. “Ah,” said Mr. Baslam. “Then you better both come in. This way.” He led them into a poky room containing four chairs, a table, and several dozen cases of stuffed animals. There was hardly room for all the cases of stuffed animals. They stood higgledy-piggledy, one on top of another, and they were all very dusty. “Sit down then,” said Mr. Baslam, rather grudgingly.

Cat sat down gently and tried not to breathe too deeply. Beside the beery smell from Mr. Baslam, there was a faint rotting smell and a smell like pickles. Cat thought that some of the stuffed animals had not been properly cured. The smell did not seem to bother Gwendolen. She sat looking like a picture of a perfect little girl. Her cream-colored dress spread crisply around her and her broad hat becomingly shaded her golden hair. She looked at Mr. Baslam with severe blue eyes.

“I think your notice is spelled wrong.”

Mr. Baslam drooped his St. Bernard eyes and made gestures that were meant to be joking. “I know. I know. But I don’t want to be taken serious, do I? Not on the very threshold, as it were. Now what was you wanting? Mr. William Nostrum don’t tell me too much of his plans. I’m only a humble supplier.”

“I want some supplies, of course,” said Gwendolen.

Cat listened, rather bored, to Gwendolen bargaining for the materials of witchcraft. Mr. Baslam fumbled in the backs of stuffed animal cases and fetched out newspaper screws of this and that—newts’ eyes, snakes’ tongues, cardamom, hellebore, mummy, niter, seed of moly, and various resins—which probably accounted for the unpleasant smell. He wanted more for them than Gwendolen would pay. She was determined to lay out her five shillings to the best possible advantage. Mr. Baslam seemed to resent it. “Know your own mind, don’t you?” he said peevishly.

“I know how much things should cost,” said Gwendolen. She took her hat off, packed the little screws of newspaper carefully into its crown, and put it neatly back on her head again. “And last, I think I shall be wanting some dragons’ blood,” she said.

“Ooooh!” said Mr. Baslam, dolefully shaking his head so that his hanging cheeks flapped. “Dragons’ blood is banned from use, young lady. You ought to know that. I don’t know as I can manage you any of that.”

“Mr. Nostrum—both Mr. Nostrums—told me you could get anything ,” said Gwendolen. “They said you were the best agent they knew. And I’m not asking for dragons’ blood now. I’m ordering some.”

Mr. Baslam looked gratified at being praised by the Nostrum brothers, but he was still dubious. “It’s a fearful strong charm needs dragons’ blood,” he said plaintively. “You won’t be doing anything that strong yourself, a young lady like you, now.”

vocabulary.definition: Feeling embarrassed or guilty about something you have done or a characteristic you have.
vocabulary.example: Cat was so ashamed that he wanted to go back and explain.

vocabulary.definition: Negotiating the terms and conditions of a purchase, agreement, or contract.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen bargaining for the materials of witchcraft.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that is appropriate and flattering.
vocabulary.example: Her broad hat becomingly shaded her golden hair.

vocabulary.definition: Having made a firm decision and being resolved not to change it.
vocabulary.example: She was determined to lay out her five shillings to the best possible advantage.

vocabulary.definition: Dark and dirty.
vocabulary.example: She hammered on the door with the dingy knocker.

vocabulary.definition: In a sorrowful or mournful manner.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Baslam said dolefully, “Dragons’ blood is banned from use.”

vocabulary.definition: Hanging or bending down.
vocabulary.example: With red, drooping eyes like a St. Bernard’s.

vocabulary.definition: Hesitating or doubting; not to be relied upon.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Baslam was still dubious about providing dragons’ blood.

vocabulary.definition: To go and bring back something.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Baslam fetched out newspaper screws of this and that.

vocabulary.definition: To make a twisted expression of pain, disgust, or disapproval.
In a reluctant or unwilling manner.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Baslam said, rather grudgingly, “Sit down then.”

vocabulary.definition: In a confused or disorderly manner.
vocabulary.example: The stuffed animals stood higgledy-piggledy, one on top of another.

vocabulary.definition: Having a low rank or position; modest.
vocabulary.example: I’m only a humble supplier.

vocabulary.definition: A written or printed announcement or warning.
vocabulary.example: An ill-written notice propped in one window: Eggsotick Serplys.

vocabulary.definition: In a bad-tempered or irritable manner.
vocabulary.example: He said peevishly, “Know your own mind, don’t you?”

vocabulary.definition: Vegetables preserved in vinegar or brine.
vocabulary.example: There was a smell like pickles in the room.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows sympathy or sorrow for someone or something.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen looked at it rather pityingly as she hammered on the door.

vocabulary.definition: Unpleasantly small and cramped.
vocabulary.example: He led them into a poky room containing four chairs.

vocabulary.definition: To support something by leaning it against something else or by putting something under it.
vocabulary.example: An ill-written notice propped in one window.

vocabulary.definition: To feel bitterness or indignation about something.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Baslam seemed to resent Gwendolen's bargaining.

vocabulary.definition: Sticky substances produced by some plants often used in making varnishes and adhesives.
vocabulary.example: Various resins probably accounted for the unpleasant smell.

vocabulary.definition: Decaying or decomposing through the action of bacteria and fungi.
vocabulary.example: There was a faint rotting smell in the room.

vocabulary.definition: To sink, bend, or hang down in the middle especially because of weight or weakness.
vocabulary.example: Old trousers which sagged to make room for his fatness.

vocabulary.definition: Very serious or strict in manner or appearance.
vocabulary.example: She looked at Mr. Baslam with severe blue eyes.

vocabulary.definition: In poor condition through long use or lack of care.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Baslam lived in a shabby cottage.

vocabulary.definition: Materials or provisions needed for a particular purpose.
vocabulary.example: I want some supplies, of course.

vocabulary.definition: The entrance to a building or the starting point of an experience.
vocabulary.example: Not on the very threshold, as it were.
